summary: Gruber details Apple's announcement of driver's license and state ID support in Apple Wallet, starting with Arizona and Georgia and initially usable at TSA checkpoints. He emphasizes the privacy-first design: users never hand over or unlock their phone, biometric authentication ensures the enrolled person is the one verifying, and the system only shares the specific data requested. He draws a parallel to Apple Pay's slow rollout and eventual dominance, predicting a similar decade-long adoption curve for digital IDs. He also praises the use of the open ISO 18013-5 standard and takes shots at impatient tech media and Google's inability to compete in mobile payments.
key_points: The digital ID system works like Apple Pay: your phone stays in your hands, never unlocks, and requires your biometric authentication to share data
The system enables selective disclosure — a bar can verify you're 21+ without seeing your birthdate, address, or other ID details, making it more private than physical IDs
Touch ID iPhones require registering a single specific finger for ID verification, ensuring the enrolled person is the one authenticating
Gruber predicts a similar adoption timeline to Apple Pay: seemingly useless for 1-2 years, growing for a few more, then default behavior within a decade
Apple's implementation uses the open ISO 18013-5 mDL standard rather than a proprietary system
money_quotes: No one should ever hand their phone to a police officer, and that goes a hundredfold if it's unlocked.
It is exceedingly more private than handing over a physical ID card, perhaps even more so than using Apple Pay compared to handing over a physical credit card.
There were a lot more complaints asking why Apple Pay didn't work almost everywhere circa 2016 than there are kudos now that it does work almost everywhere.
Patience and focus are essential to winning a long game, but success can be rather thankless. Apple excels at thankless long games.
Seriously, never ever hand your phone to a cop or anyone vaguely cop-like, like the rent-a-cops working for TSA.

My driver's license is so goddamned ugly — mostly typeset in Arial (of-fucking-course), with a script font for 'Pennsylvania' that looks like it came on a clip art CD included free with every Compaq PC in 1994.
ID cards should be beautiful and inspiring objects, a source of pride.
key_insight: Apple's digital ID system is designed with the same privacy-first, no-handover philosophy as Apple Pay, and will likely follow the same slow-but-inevitable path to ubiquity.
